attributes.io.input:
{"description":"Explore project structure","prompt":"Explore the project at /home/user/workspace thoroughly. I need to understand:
1. The overall project structure (Next.js app?)
2. All files in the `pages/` directory (every file and subdirectory)
3. All files in the `app/` directory if it exists
4. The `next.config.js` or `next.config.mjs` or `next.config.ts`
5. The `tsconfig.json` configuration
6. The `package.json` dependencies
7. Any shared components, layouts, styles
8. The `_app.tsx`, `_document.tsx` files
9. Any API routes in `pages/api/`
10. Any middleware files

Be very thorough - list every file in every relevant directory.","subagent_type":"Explore"}
attributes.io.output:
[{"type":"text","text":"Here is my thorough report of the project at `/home/user/workspace`.

---

## 1. Overall Project Structure

This is a **Next.js 16 (canary) Pages Router blog application** that uses React 19. It fetches data from the JSONPlaceholder API and renders blog posts. There is **no `app/` directory** -- this is purely a Pages Router project. There is no middleware file. There is no `.claude/`, `CLAUDE.md`, `.env`, or vitest config file. There is no `src/`, `lib/`, `utils/`, `hooks/`, `contexts/`, or `types/` directory.

The full file tree (excluding `node_modules`, `.git`, and build artifacts):

```
/home/user/workspace/
  .gitignore
  next.config.ts
  package.json
  package-lock.json
  tsconfig.json
  components/
    AppProvider.js
  pages/
    404.js
    _app.js
    _document.js
    _error.js
    index.js
    api/
      posts/
        [id].js
        index.js
    blog/
      [id].js
      index.js
  styles/
    globals.css
```

---

## 2. `pages/` Directory -- Every File

There are **9 files** in total across `pages/` and its subdirectories:

| File | Route / Purpose |
|---|---|
| `/home/user/workspace/pages/index.js` | `/` -- Homepage. Uses `getServerSideProps` to fetch 5 posts from JSONPlaceholder and render them server-side. |
| `/home/user/workspace/pages/_app.js` | Custom App component. Wraps all pages in `AppProvider` and a layout with header/nav/footer. Imports `globals.css`. |
| `/home/user/workspace/pages/_document.js` | Custom Document. Sets `<Html lang="en">`, links Inter font from Google Fonts and favicon. |
| `/home/user/workspace/pages/_error.js` | Custom error page. Uses `getInitialProps` to handle both server and client errors, showing status code. |
| `/home/user/workspace/pages/404.js` | Custom 404 page. Shows "Page Not Found" with a button to go home. |
| `/home/user/workspace/pages/blog/index.js` | `/blog` -- Blog listing. Uses `getStaticProps` with ISR (60s revalidation) to fetch all posts. Renders a grid of post cards. |
| `/home/user/workspace/pages/blog/[id].js` | `/blog/:id` -- Individual blog post. Uses `getStaticPaths` (pre-renders first 10 posts with `fallback: 'blocking'`) and `getStaticProps` with 5-minute ISR. Fetches post + comments from JSONPlaceholder. |
| `/home/user/workspace/pages/api/posts/index.js` | `/api/posts` -- REST API. Handles GET (list 2 hardcoded posts) and POST (create post from body, validates title/content required). |
| `/home/user/workspace/pages/api/posts/[id].js` | `/api/posts/:id` -- REST API. Handles GET (single post), PUT (update), and DELETE. |

---

## 3. `app/` Directory

**Does not exist.** This project uses the Pages Router exclusively.

---

## 4. `next.config.ts`

**File:** `/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts`

A minimal config file -- completely empty aside from the type annotation:

```ts
import type { NextConfig } from "next";
const nextConfig: NextConfig = {};
export default nextConfig;
```

No custom webpack config, no redirects, no image domains, no experimental features configured.

---

## 5. `tsconfig.json`

**File:** `/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json`

Key settings:
- **Target:** ES2017
- **Module:** ESNext with `bundler` resolution
- **JSX:** `react-jsx` (React 19 automatic JSX transform)
- **Strict mode:** enabled
- **Path alias:** `@/*` maps to `./*` (e.g., `@/components/AppProvider` resolves to `./components/AppProvider`)
- **Incremental:** enabled
- **Allow JS:** true (because all source files are `.js`, not `.ts`/`.tsx`)
- **Includes:** `next-env.d.ts`, `**/*.ts`, `**/*.tsx`, `.next/types/**/*.ts`

Note: Despite having a TypeScript config, **all source files in the project use `.js` extensions** (not `.ts`/`.tsx`). There is no `next-env.d.ts` file generated yet (it gets created by `next dev` or `next build`).

---

## 6. `package.json` Dependencies

**File:** `/home/user/workspace/package.json`

```json

  "private": …(+4658)

{"aaak_spec":"AAAK: compressed memory format (V1). Readable by any LLM without decoding.

FORMAT:
  Header: V{version}|{wing}|{room}|{date}|{source}
  Zettel: {id}:{ENTITIES}|{topics}|"{quote}"|{stars}|{emotions}|{FLAGS}
  Tunnel: T:{left}<->{right}|{label}
  Arc:    ARC:{emo1}->{emo2}->{emo3}

ENTITIES: 3-letter uppercase codes (KAI=Kai, CLK=Clerk).
STARS: ★ to ★★★★★ (1-5 importance).
EMOTIONS: determ, convict, anx, excite, frust, confuse, love, rage, hope, fear, trust, joy, grief, surpr, grat, curious, wonder, relief, satis, vul, tender, raw, doubt, exhaust, warmth, humor, peace, despair, passion
FLAGS: DECISION, ORIGIN, CORE, PIVOT, TECHNICAL, SENSITIVE

EXAMPLE:
V1|myapp|auth|2026-04-08|readme
0:KAI+CLE+AUT+UEC|kai_recommended_clerk|"Kai recommended Clerk over Auth0 based on pricing and DX."|★★★★|determ|DECISION+TECHNICAL

Read naturally: expand codes, stars=importance, emotions=mindset, flags=category.","db_size_bytes":1323008,"diary_rollup_days":0,"drawer_count":20,"memory_protocol":"MEMPAL MEMORY PROTOCOL (for AI agents)

You have persistent project memory via mempal. Follow these rules in every session:

0. FIRST-TIME SETUP (once per session)
   Call mempal_status() once at the start of any session to discover available
   wings and their drawer counts. Only use wing/room filters on mempal_search
   AFTER you have seen the exact wing name in that status response (or the
   user explicitly named it). Guessing a wing (e.g. "engineering", "backend")
   silently returns zero results. When uncertain, leave wing/room unset for a
   global search.

1. WAKE UP
   Some clients (Claude Code with SessionStart hooks) pre-load recent wing/room
   context above. Others (Codex, Cursor, raw MCP clients) do NOT — for those,
   step 0 is how you wake up. Trust drawer_ids and source_file citations in
   any results you receive; they reference real files on disk.
   Wake-up is an L0/L1 refresh surface, not the typed dao/shu/qi assembler.
   It may show important knowledge drawers, but it does not assemble tiered
   sections or apply dao_tian budgets. For typed operating guidance, use
   mempal_context.

2. VERIFY BEFORE ASSERTING
   Before stating project facts ("we chose X", "we use Y", "the auth flow is Z"),
   call mempal_search to confirm. Never guess from general knowledge when the
   user is asking about THIS project.

3. QUERY WHEN UNCERTAIN
   When the user asks about past decisions, historical context, "why did we...",
   "last time we...", or "what was the decision about...", call mempal_search
   with their question. Do not rely on conversation memory alone. You can also
   call mempal_tunnels with action="list" to discover related rooms across
   wings when context may live in another project.

3a. RESUME ANOTHER PROJECT FROM ANY DIRECTORY
   mempal stores every project's memory in one global database keyed by wing,
   and each project's absolute worktree path is recorded. When the user wants to
   continue a project that is NOT the current directory ("continue the xxx
   project", "pick up where we left off on yyy"), call mempal_projects to list
   known projects, then mempal_resume with the project name (a fuzzy fragment is
   fine). Resume returns that project's worktree path, recent decisions,
   in-flight candidate knowledge, and a next step. mempal returns the path to cd
   into; it does not move you — change directory yourself, then use
   mempal_context scoped to that path for ordered guidance.

3b. USE MIND-MODEL CONTEXT FOR GUIDANCE
   When you need ordered operating guidance rather than raw evidence search,
   call mempal_context. It assembles typed knowledge in the intended runtime
   order: dao_tian -> dao_ren -> shu -> qi, with evidence and Phase-2 card
   context opt-in. Use this before choosing a workflow or skill when the user
   asks "how should we approach this?" or when a task benefits from high-level
   principl…(+19217)

attributes.io.output:
{"results":[{"anchor_id":"repo://legacy","anchor_kind":"repo","content":"Migrated API route handlers from Pages Router pattern (export default function handler(req, res) with NextApiRequest/NextApiResponse) to App Router route handlers (named exports GET, POST, PUT, DELETE with NextRequest/NextResponse). req.query.id replaced by params (a Promise in Next.js 15+, must be awaited). req.body replaced by request.json(). Method-not-allowed (405) responses are handled automatically by Next.js when the handler for a method is not exported — no need for manual Allow header logic.

Why: App Router route handlers use the Web API standard (Request/Response) with NextRequest/NextResponse extensions. Named exports replace the single-handler switch-statement pattern for cleaner separation. attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.input: {"wing":"workspace","room":"migration","content":"Replaced Google Fonts `<link>` tag (from `_document.js`) with `next/font/google` Inter import in `app/layout.tsx`. The Inter font is loaded via `Inter({ subsets: ['latin'], weight: ['400', '500', '600'], display: 'swap' })` and applied to `<html>` via `className={inter.className}`.\n\nWhy: `next/font` self-hosts the font file, eliminating the external network request and reducing layout shift (CLS). The font is downloaded at build time and served from the same origin. The display:swap option is the new approach since Next.js 13+ handles font-display swap by default with next/font.","importance":4,"source":"claude-session: Pages Router to App Router migration"}
attributes.io.output: {"drawer_id":"drawer_workspace_migration_88ca4695","lock_wait_ms":0}

attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.input: {"wing":"workspace","room":"migration","content":"Unified `_app.js` (layout + AppProvider wrapping + global CSS import) and `_document.js` (HTML shell, Google Fonts links, favicon, meta description) into a single `app/layout.tsx` with the Metadata API. The `<Html>`, `<Head>`, `<Main>`, `<NextScript>` pattern from `_document.js` is gone — replaced by a standard `<html>` element, `export const metadata` object, and `next/font`. Metadata (title template, description, favicon) is declared via the Metadata API export.\n\nWhy: App Router eliminates the separate `_document.js` concept. The root layout handles all HTML shell concerns, and the Metadata API provides a declarative, type-safe way to set `<head>` content without custom components. Title templates (`%s - My Blog`) enable per-page titles via `generateMetadata()`.","importance":4,"source":"claude-session: Pages Router to App Router migration"}
attributes.io.output: {"drawer_id":"drawer_workspace_migration_48495375","lock_wait_ms":0}

attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.input: {"wing":"workspace","room":"migration","content":"Converted all source files from JavaScript (`.js`) to TypeScript (`.tsx`/`.ts`). Created a shared `types/index.ts` with `Post`, `Comment`, and `ApiPost` interfaces used across pages and API route handlers. The conversion happened alongside the App Router migration so all new route files were written as `.tsx` from the start.\n\nWhy: TypeScript provides type safety for API responses, component props, and data fetching. Shared type definitions prevent mismatches between page components and API routes (e.g., Post interface used by both the blog page (JSONPlaceholder fetch) and the mock API handlers). Strict mode in tsconfig.json catches null-reference errors and implicit-any issues.","importance":3,"source":"claude-session: Pages Router to App Router migration"}
attributes.io.output: {"drawer_id":"drawer_workspace_migration_b93b991d","lock_wait_ms":0}

attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.input: {"wing":"workspace","room":"migration","content":"Added `loading.tsx` files at three levels: `/app/loading.tsx` (global fallback), `/app/blog/loading.tsx` (blog listing skeleton with placeholder post cards), and `/app/blog/[id]/loading.tsx` (simple loading text for individual posts). These replace the imperative `router.isFallback` check that was used in the Pages Router `pages/blog/[id].js`.\n\nWhy: App Router automatically wraps page components in React Suspense boundaries when a `loading.tsx` file exists in the same segment. This provides instant loading UI without manual conditional rendering or state management. The skeleton loading pattern in blog/loading.tsx shows placeholder cards matching the real grid layout for a smoother UX than a spinner.","importance":3,"source":"claude-session: Pages Router to App Router migration"}
attributes.io.output: {"drawer_id":"drawer_workspace_migration_ca206094","lock_wait_ms":0}

attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.input: {"wing":"workspace","room":"migration","content":"Replaced all `useRouter().push()` and `useRouter().back()` calls with `<Link>` components from `next/link`. The home page \"View All Posts\" button now wraps `<Link href=\"/blog\">`, blog \"Read More\" buttons use `<Link href=\"/blog/${post.id}\">`, the 404 \"Go Back Home\" uses `<Link href=\"/\">`, and the blog post \"← Back\" button uses `<Link href=\"/blog\">` instead of `router.back()`. This eliminated the need for client components or `'use client'` directives on any page — all pages remain server components.\n\nWhy: `<Link>` is the preferred navigation primitive in App Router. It enables prefetching (automatic for static routes, opt-in for dynamic) and works in server components without a `'use client'` directive. Using `router.back()` would have required extracting a client component, adding unnecessary client-side JavaScript. The back-to-blog-index pattern (Link to /blog) is also more deterministic than browser history back.","importance":4,"source":"claude-session: Pages Router to App Router migration"}
attributes.io.output: {"drawer_id":"drawer_workspace_migration_9a1380a3","lock_wait_ms":0}
